reviews.review_description: its alright i guess. apart from the swinging snd collision, it needs more content after you save her, maybe a mode where both cleric and player gonon missions?

reviews.review_title: Please for your sanity dont play this.
reviews.review_description:
The game is clunky, boring, and slow. Your only viable method is standing still just barely outside the goblin's attack range and just.. poking them in the face with a spear.
Any other playstyle is discouraged as when you drop a weapon for more than 20 seconds, it despawns, swinging forces you to let go of your weapon 30% of the time, and slashing is incredibly useless.

reviews.review_title: You'll die if you use speed or force.
reviews.review_description: Counterintuitively to most VR games, the hit detection doesn't require swinging your weapon with force. Have you seen the Walking Dead TV show? You want to gradually sink your blade into the goblin's head or heart. Press A to flip your weapon downward and when it sticks to the goblin's head, drive your arm down steadily like cutting through a watermelon. Don't be afraid to use your other hand to hold goblin still. Armored enemies are tougher and may need  to be knocked around.

reviews.review_title: Most of the time you can't get anywhere!
reviews.review_description:
I have tried this multiple times but it seems the same thing happens everytime.....except one rare time.
After the "Made with Unity" screen, there sounds like a single knock while the screen is pure black, then it slowly, and I mean very slowly and choppy, it trys to load the tavern, not all the area loads, and is very janky to unplayable when you try to move, I usually have to quit and give up.

It doesn't make sense, I own a Meta Quest 3, and much more demanding games graphically run just fine!  I never had this problem on the demo which I thoroughly enjoyed and was why I purchased this.

Please optimize your game so I can play it and I'll give you a higher Star rating!
Thanks for your time and understanding. 🙏

This game has a good mix of procedural and immersive take in a anime-fantasy. We don't have many anime fantasy VR games.

The positives of the game is that it doesn't hold your hand. Battles are very unpredictable at times where you can get your weapons swatted aside and must think on your feet like a movie. The goblins, though small, can easily take you out within a few attacks. I like the unsettling quiet and the overall emptiness of the dungeon, making you feel so isolated and even fearful if you dial the brightness down.

If there is a negative I would say the only real negative is a lack of a story and interaction with Cleric. The other is sometimes the physics act up, where the goblins skulls act like steel armor or weapons fly away despite no force applied to deflecting your blows.

The game starts off each time with stealth first. You need to be careful as to how you handle goblins so you have enough health to save Cleric. Once Cleric is free the game shifts where you're feeling invincible and carefree, since Cleric is consistently healing you.

I like the little tavern at the end of each level, I wish it was occupied with more, people, girls that we can save or fight alongside with. I see potential in this setup for more story based or roguelite games with more enemies/goblin types to face, more weapons with unique enchantments, different types of dungeons, bosses and more story involving Cleric or even more heroines to meet and save. (Would be cool if we had different classes or types of heroines, starting with Cleric herself, and a warrior and even a sorcerer as the difficulty ramps up. Maybe even have an upgrade system.)

Exploring and hunting missions could be used. And you can bring along Cleric.

I would love for an update for this game. It's straight to the point and has a good repetitiveness that immerses you despite the first glance of the game. I actually have successfully saved Cleric with no damage and now she's safe at the tavern.

The collision detection is practically make believe.
After playing Sword and Sorcery, and Swordsman, I expected at least SOME realism in the weapons handling and combat. Hitting the enemies are seemingly up to some kind of invisible Dice Roll system, because you can walk up and stick a blade into a goblin and it will not register anything except your own disbelief that nothing happened after inserting a blade handle deep into some goblin ribs....for zero damage of course.

This seems like a very easy problem to fix, considering these dingle berries also did Sword and Sorcery.

Must add more stuff 
Like goblin stashes chests 
Store mechanic 
And need to learn the health bar not sure other then last two hits before faint
Must add nood mod 
This game has a lot of potential 
And could make one where player is goblin raiding villages and kidnapping girls

Do you Love Dungeon Exploration, Immersive Ambiance, Weapon Collecting, Physics Driven Combat & Anime Waifus?

 Say No More Fam! 

For me this Never gets Old. It has a Replayability similar to the randomly Generated Levels in Dread Halls & I highly Recommend! 😎👍

reviews.review_title: The most interesting game I've played
reviews.review_description:
Oh boy where do I start. Cleric and Goblins was a game I have no history on in terms of ambition to play, its a dungeon crawler with by far the hardest gameplay in my experience.

In terms of the overall combat, the goblin enemies are very varied, they are literal statues when you try and attack them, on another note it is hard to determine when you are dealing damage, the physics work okay, armor and weapon collision need no prior input. Sometimes direct hits from blades will bounce right off as if their skin was steel.

The weaponry slip out of your hand from fast movement or minor bumps in terrain which is profoundly irritating, also makes no sense when you can professionally flip bladed weapons with ease. However it is a great feature if balanced out more, perhaps enemies can disarm you instead or swinging too much will loosen grip.

 The weight of long weaponry is extremely heavy, needs more compensation.

The physical gripping and force grip is very wonky, you cannot reach fully to the ground no matter the height setting which aces difficulty when picking up items in certain conditions. The force grab ability needs close proximity and precise targeting or perhaps it is not configured correctly.

The cleric, a stressful and obnoxious figure. I am completely clueless on the nature of games like these but the cleric appears too unprincipled, she's pretty much invincible which makes her less of a priority, the goals and logic are in the sky and she makes really weird noises. The cleric walks out the tavern, gets kidnapped forcing the protagonist (player) to return the favour by rescuing the cleric. This scenario repeats in increasing difficulty.

 The gameplay needs variety, maybe traps and different enemies can be present in dungeons, a bit of story and reason can bring customers to invest in the game. There could be an upgrade system in which you increase strength, defence, armour, weaponry, abilities, etc.

What is good about the game is the simplistic idea overall, the challenge is there and it is possible to win. You can use coins as landmarks, perhaps there should be a currency which is affected by said landmark feature. The adrenaline when the cleric is rescued and are both tasked with a hasty exit is awesome, the music and the potential power of constant regeneration brings out an inner berserk.

With absolute determination this game can go places, and I just realised I wrote an unnecessarily long review. Do not take the cons into heart because I have hopes for this.

The more I play this game the more I appreciate it. I can understand there being some low star reviews by people who didn’t play long enough or perhaps just missed the point of the game. At first the weapons seemed difficult to handle and keep hold of. And sharp ones can stick for a while in the goblins. But I learned that they can be picked up again easily at a distance with the grasp triggers and turned around in a hand when necessary by pressing A or X. (The weapons that another reviewer complained flopped around like noodles are actually long two-handed weapons that can indeed sometimes flop around if held in one hand—but they are still pretty effective when used that way.) Things will go wrong, especially in the hectic later parts of the game. But for me that is just part of the challenge and the charm of this game.

Fighting the goblins is very tactical and the wounds on them accumulate. A particular tactic I’d recommend is to lure individual goblins into tunnels away from their companions as much as possible to fight them on their own. Some of the more beautiful weapons found deeper in the caves are also particularly effective ones.

There are lots of nice touches like different food each time in the tavern from which each adventure starts. There’s a neat medieval town that you can see through the windows if you happen to get close to them. Very atmospheric caves, differently arranged every time. The goblins’ victim you are finding and rescuing is nice indeed and restores health magically during your remaining fights to get out of the cave after you find her. She does trip herself up and fall over a lot—even more than the goblins. Be patient with her.

The structure of the game is great. In the first bit of the game you are rescued in the woods by the magical woman that you will later be rescuing in the caves, and then you find yourself in a tavern. On a bulletin board is a poster saying she has gone missing. You click on the poster and are in a complex and moody goblin cave looking for the woman. If you die from your injuries in fighting goblins, you find yourself back in the tavern and can start again by clicking the poster again.

The caves are arranged differently each time you go into them. Initially the poster has a single star on it. If the rescue is successful, you return in triumph to the tavern with the woman who then leaves. When you look at the poster again you see that she needs rescuing again. But this time there are two stars on the poster and the cave will be even bigger, more interesting and more challenging. With four stars (for me only after weeks of playing) the game is awe-inspiringly challenging. The caves are enormous and elaborate. I don’t know how many further levels there are—but this game sure has great longevity.

The developers seem to pay a lot of attention to the game. I’ve seen them add practice at fighting goblins, new weapons and increased numbers of health potion flasks to be found in the difficult later cave systems.

The combat is bad, really bad. The weapons get stuck on things, fall out of your hand randomly and are very hard to pick back up. 

The combat is hard to tell if you are hitting the enemy or not, also its hard to tell if you are taking damage. Feels like you are fighting with pool noodles.

Sound is bad, just random grunts and moans, background does not have immersive sounds.

Graphics are not bad. All the details and physics went into the look and jiggle of the Cleric.

The immersion is just not there, you just don't get drawn into this world.

Fun Factor, fail. You play dungeon after dungeon that look pretty much the same. It starts with the Cleric leaving the tavern, you hear her get captured. Then you enter a dungeon, find her and walk her out. Rinse and repeat the exact same routine.

I give this one a 2.5 / 5. Needs work and polish, not ready for sale.

Watch my Quick Look here,
https://youtube.com/watch?v=kUySsUxbk1A&si=EnSIkaIECMiOmarE

I enjoyed the time I had with this but found it way to hard to get anywhere. 

The graphics are great and probably the best thing about the game. The ragdoll effects are on overload and when u hit enemies u aint really sure if u hit them or not. 

With only one health potion its difficult to last long. I found the weapons fly out of your hands to easy also. 

With a few tweaks this could be a great game https://youtu.be/N1I3WzI3vL8

What an absolutely fantastic game !!  The sense of place is crazy good.  The fighting can be frick'en hilarious from time to time and the sound is great too !  A few things that would definitely improve it...
Higher res textures, more level variety.  (Sone of which could be partially done by simple palette changes.)  Getting your coins to mark your way through by finding them in hidden chests.  
(The way it is now you get an unlimited amount of them and you don't have to find them.)
That part should be harder.
Sometimes, connected segments don't lineup correctly. 
And the textures used in the tavern should have been made with 3d images.  In fact all the textures should be from 3d images.  That being said this game is still FANTASTIC !!!
In the PCVR version, there are multi languages. In Quest stand-alone version, can we switch to different languages? Thanks.

reviews.review_title: Some minor issues, but a fun game.
reviews.review_description:
Saw this game on Twitter, love the idea. Been playing it and I'm having fun with the gameplay loop where you pick a difficulty, traverse a randomly generated dungeon and fight goblins to free said cleric, then go back the way you came with her, fighting goblins on the way.

Combat can be frustrating at first as blows can be hit-or-miss, possibly due to the natural angle of the Quest controller making blades be slightly off, the dungeon traversal is fun but you can get lost and no marker system can make running in and out a chore. Enemies can pile up or run at you and you can take cheap hits due to the physics system. It's sometimes hard to tell if you're about to die as combat can obscure the red mist and heartbeat with sounds. And I've been pushed into rocks with no way out or had my character's arms disappear, ending runs.

That said, the escorting part of the game is thankfully easy as the cleric is invincible and heals you after every encounter if you've taken hits. That said, she doesn't fight and can get locked if goblins are nearby, so stay close.

It's a fun game otherwise and once you get things down, it's a fun romp killing goblins. That said, it would be nice to see some more indications of progress aside from the stars on the poster going up.

Some tips I found were:
-Easiest way to kill a goblin with the dagger you start is wait until they taunt you, grab their heads, and stab them there. The dagger could bounce off, but it's easier to do with reversing the grip by pressing A.
-The dagger doesn't disappear, so you can use it as your one and only marker.
-Blunt weapons are easier to use and knock armor off so stick with them if you're having trouble.
-Remember that the right stick is a teleport in default settings, which can get you out of sticky situations by passing through them.

There are other tips, but I'll leave that to prospective players. Give this one a shot. It got me back into playing with the Quest and at 10 dollars, I think it was worth it.
If you can make it a longer game and add more enemies, environments and avatars if necessary, Thanks

Not a easy game to play. No easy level. But very enjoyable because of the challenges.  Learning what certain pickups will do like the the reddish looking bars are lives. ,and you have to smash them to the ground for them to work.  Perhaps a easy level may help gamers a bit more to get into it. The graphics I found were very impressive for a quest game. Well done the devs
If you can be so kind to make the goblins less aggressive that will be much appreciated and can you put an easy normal and hard settings in as well thank you
